The config factory returns the shared DEFAULTS dictionary by reference, so callers accidentally mutate global defaults when applying overrides.

The update logic fails to keep display_name synchronized with name when the name field changes.

The profile updater updates first_name without recomputing full_name, leaving the derived field stale when only the first name changes.

The email update path changes the stored email but fails to reset verification state and clear the cached greeting, so stale greeting data can persist after an email change.

The cache is never invalidated or updated when a product changes, so get_product() can return stale data after update_product().

The host value is captured once at import time in a separate global, so later calls to configure() or reset_settings() do not affect get_host().

The client caches the config at import time, so resetting or updating the config later does not refresh the client’s timeout value.

The client captures the config dictionary at import time, so after reset_config() creates a fresh config object, the handler still reads stale values through the old reference.

The bug is caused by using a mutable list as a default argument in enqueue(). That list is created once at function definition time and then reused across calls, so tasks accumulate unexpectedly betwe
The queue function uses a mutable default list, causing tasks to persist across separate calls instead of starting fresh.

The bug is caused by using a mutable default argument for seen in process_batch. That set persists across calls, so tasks processed in one batch are incorrectly treated as duplicates in later batches.
The batch processor uses a mutable default set for `seen`, causing task IDs from earlier calls to persist and incorrectly skip valid tasks in later batches.

The batch processor updates the counter for all items first and records only one snapshot at the end, so it fails to capture per-item state changes.

The batch processor emits only one event after the loop instead of emitting one event per item, so event counts can never match processed items.

The function reuses a global cached list and returns it unchanged for falsy/empty input, so empty calls incorrectly expose stale results instead of a fresh empty list.

The pipeline reuses stale state by falling back to _last_best when no current record matches, so find_best returns an old record instead of None.

The retry loop ignores successful sends because it continues only on failure and never stops early, while clear_log is also unimplemented.

The retry loop keeps calling send() after the first successful attempt because there is no success exit condition, causing duplicate appends and notifications.

The pipeline-level retry loop always continues even after a successful send, causing the same message to be sent multiple times and duplicated in the store.

The order flow re-raises wallet charging errors without releasing the previously reserved inventory, leaving stock incorrectly locked after a failed payment.

Payment failures re-raise without releasing the prior inventory reservation, leaving stock permanently marked as reserved.

execute_transfer mixes transfer state changes with logging/alerting, making the business flow hard to read and preventing a clean separation between core transfer logic and observability.

Removing commit() and freeze_view() left the pipeline in a non-frozen, unstabilized state, so committed selectors return None and the materialized view no longer reflects canonical committed data.

The API writes the same product twice via redundant fresh and cached writers, and the second write can overwrite versioned data with unversioned cached data, causing stale or inconsistent reads.

The bug is a classic lost-update race caused by splitting increment into separate read and write steps without synchronization. Under interleaving, both operations can capture the same old value and t

The counter loses updates because increment is implemented as a non-atomic read-modify-write split across interleavable steps, so concurrent increments can overwrite each other with the same stale val

The bug is a classic non-atomic check-then-act race: each withdrawal checks the balance independently, so two interleaved withdrawals can both observe the initial sufficient balance and both debit aft
The withdrawal uses a non-atomic check-then-act sequence, so two interleaved withdrawals can both pass the balance check before either decrements the account.

The bug is that process() assumes init() has already run and drops any item that arrives earlier. To make processing order-independent, items must be buffered until initialization occurs, then flushed
process() drops items received before init() because it returns early instead of buffering them for later processing.

The deadlock is caused by inconsistent lock acquisition order: A→B acquires A then B, while B→A acquires B then A. Under interleaving, each transfer can hold one lock and wait forever for the other. T
Transfers deadlock because the two transfer paths acquire shared resource locks in opposite orders, allowing circular wait under interleaving.
